title: देवी सूक्तम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री देवी सूक्तम् ॥

ॐ अहं रुद्रेभिर्वसुभिश्चराम्यहमादित्यैरुत विश्वदेवैः।
अहं मित्रावरुणोभा बिभर्म्यहमिन्द्राग्नी अहमश्विनोभा॥१॥

अहं सोममाहनसं बिभर्म्यहं त्वष्टारमुत पूषणं भगम्।
अहं दधामि द्रविणं हविष्मते सुप्राव्ये यजमानाय सुन्वते॥२॥

अहं राष्ट्री संगमनी वसूनां चिकितुषी प्रथमा यज्ञियानाम्।
तां मा देवा व्यदधुः पुरुत्रा भूरिस्थात्रां भूर्यावेशयन्तीम्॥३॥

मया सो अन्नमत्ति यो विपश्यति यः प्राणिति य ईं शृणोत्युक्तम्।
अमन्तवो मां त उप क्षियन्ति श्रुधि श्रुत श्रद्धिवं ते वदामि॥४॥

अहमेव स्वयमिदं वदामि जुष्टं देवेभिरुत मानुषेभिः।
यं कामये तं तमुग्रं कृणोमि तं ब्रह्माणं तमृषिं तं सुमेधाम्॥५॥

अहं रुद्राय धनुरा तनोमि ब्रह्मद्विषे शरवे हन्तवा उ।
अहं जनाय समदं कृणोम्यहं द्यावापृथिवी आ विवेश॥६॥

अहं सुवे पितरमस्य मूर्धन्मम योनिरप्स्वन्तः समुद्रे।
ततो वि तिष्ठे भुवनानु विश्वोतामूं द्यां वर्ष्मणोप स्पृशामि॥७॥

अहमेव वात इव प्र वाम्यारभमाणा भुवनानि विश्वा।
परो दिवा पर एना पृथिव्यैतावती महिना सं बभूव॥८॥

॥ इति ऋग्वेद दशम मण्डल श्री देवी सूक्तं सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Devi Suktam ||

Om Aham Rudrebhir Vasubhish Charamy Aham Adityair Uta Vishvadevaiah.
Aham Mitravaruno'bha Bibharmy Aham Indragni Aham Ashvino'bha.||1||

Aham Somam Ahanasam Bibharmy Aham Tvashtaram Uta Pushanam Bhagam.
Aham Dadhami Dravinam Havishmate Supravye Yajamanaya Sunvate.||2||

Aham Rashtri Sangamani Vasunam Chikitushi Prathama Yajniyanam.
Tam Ma Deva Vyadadhuh Purutra Bhuristhatram Bhuryaveshayantim.||3||

Maya So Annam Atti Yo Vipashyati Yah Praniti Ya Im Shrinoty Uktam.
Amantavo Mam Ta Upa Kshiyanti Shrudhi Shruta Shraddhivam Te Vadami.||4||

Aham Eva Svayam Idam Vadami Jushtam Devebhir Uta Manushebhih.
Yam Kamaye Tam Tam Ugram Krinomi Tam Brahmanam Tam Rishim Tam Sumedham.||5||

Aham Rudraya Dhanur A Tanomi Brahmadvise Sharave Hantava U.
Aham Janaya Samadam Krinomy Aham Dyavaprithivi A Vivesha.||6||

Aham Suve Pitaram Asya Murdhan Mama Yonir Apsv Antah Samudre.
Tato Vi Tishthe Bhuvananu Vishvota Mum Dyam Varshmano'pa Sprishami.||7||

Aham Eva Vata Iva Pra Vamy Arabhamana Bhuvanani Vishva.
Paro Diva Para Ena Prithivyai Tavati Mahina Sam Babhuva.||8||

|| Iti Rigveda Dashama Mandala Shri Devi Suktam Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
मैं (देवी) रुद्रों, वसुओं, आदित्यों और विश्वदेवों के साथ विचरण करती हूँ। मैं मित्र और वरुण दोनों को धारण करती हूँ। मैं इन्द्र और अग्नि को, तथा दोनों अश्विनीकुमारों को धारण करती हूँ।

मैं सोम को, त्वष्टा को, पूषा को और भग को धारण करती हूँ। मैं हवि देने वाले, उत्तम रक्षण करने वाले, यजमान और सोम निकालने वाले को धन प्रदान करती हूँ।

मैं राष्ट्र की स्वामिनी हूँ, धनों को एकत्र करने वाली हूँ, ज्ञानवती हूँ, यज्ञीय देवताओं में प्रथम हूँ। देवताओं ने मुझे अनेक स्थानों में स्थापित किया है।

मैं ही स्वयं यह कहती हूँ, जो देवताओं और मनुष्यों को प्रिय है। जिसे मैं चाहती हूँ उसे उग्र बनाती हूँ, उसे ब्रह्मज्ञानी, ऋषि और सुमेधावी बनाती हूँ।

मैं वायु के समान सभी भुवनों को आरम्भ करती हुई बहती हूँ। मेरी महिमा द्युलोक से परे और पृथ्वी से परे है - इतनी विशाल मैं हुई हूँ।
benefits: Contains the primordial revelation of the Divine Feminine (Shakti)
Invokes knowledge of the supreme cosmic power of the goddess
Invokes the blessing of sovereignty, wisdom, and spiritual illumination
Invokes the all-pervading presence of the Devi
One of the most ancient and powerful Vedic hymns to the Goddess
Invokes strength, valor, and divine protection
description: Dedicated to Goddess Durga, the Devi Suktam (देवी सूक्तम्) is a Vedic hymn revered in Hindu tradition. Drawn from Rigveda (10.125), this text has been cherished by devotees for centuries. Regular recitation is believed to grant knowledge of the supreme cosmic power of the Goddess, bestow sovereignty, wisdom, and spiritual illumination, and contain the primordial revelation of the Divine Feminine (Shakti). This prayer is traditionally recited on Tuesday (Mangalvar) and Friday (Shukravar) and during Navratri to invoke divine grace. With 8 verses and a reading time of about 5 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Devi Suktam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Devi Suktam from Rigveda in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning and transliteration. Ancient Vedic hymn revealing the supreme power of the Goddess.

Durga Saptashati murti rahasya Devi Sukta

Devi Sukta of Devi Mahatmya 8 verses
Brahmanda jagadishvari Aham Sukta Veda mantras

seoFaqs.question: What is the Devi Suktam and which Vedic source does it come from?
seoFaqs.answer: Devi Suktam (देवी सूक्तम्), also called the Vak Suktam or Vak-Ambhrini Suktam, is one of the most theologically extraordinary hymns in the entire Rigveda — Mandala 10, Sukta 125, with eight mantras. Its mantra-drashtri (seer) was the Rishika Vak, daughter of Sage Ambhrina (hence Vak-Ambhrini), one of the few female seers (rishikas) credited with composing a complete Rigvedic sukta. The composition is in the first person — the Devi speaks autobiographically as 'I am' — declaring her identity with the Vedic devas (Rudras, Vasus, Adityas, Vishvadevas, Mitra-Varuna, Indra-Agni, the Ashvins, Soma, Tvashtri, Pushan, Bhaga), as the Rashtri (sovereign), as the wealth-giver, as the speech that knowers know, and as the cosmic wind that exists beyond heaven and earth. (Sources: Rigveda 10.125; Sayana Bhashya; Brihaddevata.)

seoFaqs.question: Who is Vak-Ambhrini and what is her significance as a female Vedic seer?
seoFaqs.answer: Vak (literally 'speech', cognate with Latin 'vox') is one of the most ancient personifications of the cosmic creative principle in the Veda. As a rishika, she is named Ambhrini — the daughter of the rishi Ambhrina. The Brihaddevata records her as the one who 'beheld' (drashtum) this hymn, making her one of approximately twenty-seven rishikas of the Rigveda (alongside Lopamudra, Ghosha, Apala, Vishvavara, Sikata-Nivavari and others). Her hymn is unique in that it is not addressed to a deity — it is the deity speaking through her in first person. The Devi Bhagavata identifies Vak with Adi Parashakti herself, and later traditions equate her with Saraswati (the goddess of speech and wisdom), with Durga (in the Devi Mahatmya), and with Mahalakshmi (in some Vaishnava commentaries). She is the prototype of every later 'I am the Goddess' revelation. (Sources: Brihaddevata; Devi Bhagavata; Sayana Bhashya.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Aham Rashtri Sangamani Vasunam?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 3 of the Devi Suktam contains the famous declaration: 'Aham Rashtri Sangamani Vasunam, Chikitushi Prathama Yajniyanam, Tam Ma Deva Vyadadhuh Purutra, Bhuristhatram Bhuryaveshayantim' — 'I am the Sovereign (Rashtri), the gatherer-together of all wealth (vasus), the knower (chikitushi), foremost of those worthy of yajna; the devas have established me in many places, with many abodes, entering into many beings'. The word 'Rashtri' — feminine of Rashtra (nation, kingdom) — establishes the Devi as the supreme political-cosmic sovereign; this single line is one of the most-cited Vedic verses in Indian feminist theology and in classical statecraft alike. The verse continues that all the devas have placed the Devi in countless sthanas (places, forms, beings) — affirming her omnipresence. (Sources: Rigveda 10.125.3; Sayana Bhashya; Brihaddevata.)

seoFaqs.question: How does the Devi Suktam fit into the Durga Saptashati?
seoFaqs.answer: Within the Durga Saptashati (Devi Mahatmya from the Markandeya Purana), the Vedic Devi Suktam is recited at the very end as part of the closing parayana, demonstrating that the Devi of the Saptashati's three caritas (Madhu-Kaitabha, Mahishasura, Shumbha-Nishumbha) is none other than the cosmic Vak who declared her own identity in the Rigveda's Mandala 10. This integration of Vedic and Puranic streams — placing a Rigvedic sukta inside a Puranic parayana — is unique to the Saptashati and one of its most theologically sophisticated features. The Saptashati's commentators (especially Bhaskararaya, Nagoji Bhatta) explain that without the Devi Sukta, the Saptashati's chanting is incomplete; the puranic narrative is grounded in the Vedic self-revelation. (Sources: Markandeya Purana Devi Mahatmya; classical Saptashati paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the difference between Devi Suktam and Sri Suktam?
seoFaqs.answer: Both are Vedic suktas to the Goddess but differ significantly. Sri Suktam (Khila Sukta of the Rigveda, 15-16 verses) is specifically a propitiation hymn to Sri-Lakshmi as goddess of material and spiritual wealth, with detailed iconography (golden, lotus-bearing, accompanied by elephants, riding on a lotus) and is recited primarily at Lakshmi-puja, Diwali and for prosperity. Devi Suktam (Rigveda 10.125, 8 verses) is by contrast a non-iconographic, philosophical self-declaration of the Goddess as the universal Vak/Shakti without specific form-attributes — it is the Vedanta-foundation for Shakta theology, recited primarily at Navratri and as Saptashati anga. Sri Suktam = wealth-form propitiation; Devi Suktam = supreme-shakti realisation. They complement each other in the complete Goddess upasana. (Sources: Rigveda Khila; classical paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: When and how should the Devi Suktam be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: Devi Suktam is recited at: (1) Each of the nine nights of Sharad Navratri (Ashvin Shukla Pratipada to Navami) and Vasanta Navratri (Chaitra Shukla) — typically 9 times across the nine days as part of the full Saptashati anga or 1 time daily. (2) Tuesday (Mangalvar) and Friday (Shukravar) — the two weekdays sacred to the Devi. (3) Krishna Paksha Ashtami of every month. (4) Diwali night for Mahalakshmi. (5) Daily by Shakta sadhakas as part of nityapaath. (6) After the principal Saptashati parayana as the closing 'rahasya'. The recitation should be in proper Vedic svara (with correct udatta, anudatta and svarita accents) — for which the audio of a trained Vedapathin should be referenced. The mantra-japa count for siddhi is traditionally 1,25,000 (one purascharana). (Sources: classical Shakta paddhati; Saptashati paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does the Devi Suktam bestow?
seoFaqs.answer: Traditional benefits enumerated by Saptashati commentators and Shakta paddhati include: (a) sovereignty in one's domain — career, household, leadership (echoing 'Aham Rashtri'); (b) wealth and resources (echoing 'Sangamani Vasunam'); (c) knowledge and wisdom — the Devi makes whomever she chooses a brahmajnani, rishi or sumedha (verse 5); (d) protection from enemies and evil forces (verse 6 — 'I bend the bow for Rudra against the haters of Brahman'); (e) freedom from fear — those who 'do not heed her' decay (verse 4); (f) success in any undertaking begun with this recitation; (g) merit equivalent to study of the entire Rigveda when chanted with shraddha and proper svara; (h) ultimately, realisation that one is not separate from the Devi who pervades 'beyond heaven and beyond earth' (verse 8). (Sources: Saptashati commentaries; classical Shakta phalashruti.)
